So, page four is sort of your master list and it is on the back of your little, your cheat sheet card. So if you still have your card, it should be on the back side of it. And we will start right with number one. So number one is really about clarity and are you going to make a liar out of me or is it on the back of your tin? Okay. Good. So it's interesting to me that most marketers and I find most copywriters don't even have this stage. They skip this stage. Is there really not clear about their overarching objectives that they have to achieve with their targets in order for anything to really be effective? So the example I gave you here is from the financial services industry, the four things that would be overarching objectives that they have to achieve for them to be able to achieve much of anything else. So these are stockbrokers, investment newsletter, publishers, anybody dealing with you in any way, shape or form about where you ought to put some money. They have to have you active. A passive investor is no good to them because they want you moving money. So if you have money under management and you don't move any of it, the guy makes a point or two. If you move it a lot, he might make eight, nine, ten, he'll make more on your money that year than you will make on your money, which is exactly their objective. I'm not even passing judgment. I'm just telling you what it is. So they want you to be an active investor. Secondly, they want you to act based on short-term information because that makes you more active. So oh, crap, tech stocks are rising. I got to take some money out of P&G and I got to buy some tech stocks. Oh, shit, tech stocks are crashing. I got to take some money out of my tech stocks and I got to move them over here before noon tomorrow into something else. That's the mindset they want you. They want to create fear and anxiety because fear and anxiety causes people to move more than does anything else. So if you turn in Fox Financial and you see that there's a gradual upswing trend in direct sales company stocks, you are not going to run necessarily to the phone to call your guy and say, I got to get 20 grand in Herbalife right now. If you happen to hold Herbalife and you tune into Fox and there's three experts on there talking about any minute there could be a huge correction in direct sales company stocks and they're all going to crash, you are on the phone. So they want a constant fear and anxiety. By the way, if you clock it, you could even do it with one show. But if you watch, if you can bring yourself to do it, if you watch like any three nights of Kramer, you know, the money guy, you know, I'm right on, if you watch any three nights and you clock it, the ratio is very consistent. One third of the show is about things going up, two thirds of the show is about things going down. And so he is helping the cause. They want to tempt you with new products that appear to offer increased returns without added risk, upside with no downside. And you will see the industry constantly creating this stuff or repositioning stuff they've got to fit this, right? So they will select right now, regardless of what you think about precious metals, it's a funny commercial to me. So the silver commercial, pay attention the next time you're, so here's the pitch in the silver commercial. Silver has lost so much value and they actually show you the chart over like the last decade that you've got to buy silver now. This is the pitch as if they weren't telling you to buy silver back here. Of course they were, they sell silver. So if you paid any attention to their advice, here, here, here, here, or here, you're bleeding out your rectum. But now pay attention to my advice because it finally has crashed so badly. I made this mistake once with bank stocks. Like an idiot. I'll never forget it. I'm sitting and it's Carlos Fault. Because I'm sitting in the Concierge lounge at Boardwalk at Disney and she's already supposed to have been here. We're supposed to be on our way and they have the financial news show on and there's four smart guys all saying bank stocks cannot go any lower. The same is the silver, it's the silver commercial. It's impossible. They have just hit rock bottom. She's a half an hour late and I've bought 50 grand worth of bank stocks. And guess what? There's no bottom. They just kept moving. By the time we left Disney, I'm down twice that. You know, I'm going, ugh, ugh, ugh. Carlos is going. You know, you're paying a lot of money for those shirts for the kids. You know, 15 bucks is what I'm going. You have no idea. So you need this short list. You need this short list for your market and really for every time you write copy of what are the three or four or five short is key overarching things that we have to make happen with these people for anything else we want to do to work. And then you got to make sure that everything you're doing is facilitating and supporting your short list. And that short list is about their psyche, about their mindset, about where they have to be mentally and emotionally to respond to the specifics of any pitch. Now it can be about the way you want them to think or the way you don't want them to think, what you want to banish from thought can be either or both. But you got to have clarity on it, right? So I probably irritated him a little, but I would assume so, I would assume Nelson would agree with this. They in selling Christianity do not want you having any positive thoughts whatsoever about reincarnation. This is not helpful probably to their cause. And it might well be particularly if they are targeting, let's say they were going to target metaphysical believers in the area of their church and try and move them to the church, on their short list would be getting these people not to think about the fact that they get to come back 22 times until they get it right. Because they kind of need you to think you got to get it right right now or you're going to be down there with Lucifer in the heat. Visi versa by the way. So everybody needs a short list. What can't we have them think? And what must we have them think? So I get the kid to buy braces, see the big issue is going to be delay. You know, this person has got to think this gets worse and this gets more expensive if we wait for six months. Oh, we're all going on vacation to Europe for three weeks. We'll wait until we come back from the vacation. You know, there's a lot of reasons to wait. Right? They've got to believe this is like, you know, cancer, a crooked teeth. I mean, this is getting worse by the day and it's going to be worse to fix and more painful to fix and more expensive to fix every minute you wait. It's like a fire alarm sale. They've got to think tonight could be the night. Or you know, well, we're going to remodel the kitchen anyway. We'll do the fire alarm system when we remodel the kitchen. See, they've got to think tonight could be the night. You know, and that's on the shortlist because everybody believes A, it only happens in somebody else's house and B, it's not going to happen now. Right? That's what everybody believes. Right? And statistics alone are not, you know, so, you know, the statistics alone are not persuasive. So the statistic is everybody will have at least one home fire during their lifetime. That's the statistic. But now, do you believe it's going to happen to you tonight? By the way, my little fire alarm story and I get no commissions, but just for your benefit. How many of you have a smoke detector? You all got smoke detectors, right? It's been hoisted on you. How many have heat detectors? Aha! So here's five minutes that has nothing to do with what we're doing, but it's advice for you. Go get heat detectors. Two-thirds of the fires, by the time there's enough smoke to set the smoke detector off you're dead. Right? And the entire house is in flames. Right? Smoke detectors don't detect heat. They only detect smoke. That's why they're called a smoke detector. However, the sprinkler systems in this hotel and in almost every commercial building by law, they're heat detectors. They're not smoke detectors. There's a reason for it. So the thing that sets off the sprinkler, that's a heat detector. Right? And it works just like the heat detector you can buy for your house. And it's the lowest tech item in the world. It doesn't wire into anything. It's a painted metal can. It's about this big. It's got a big metal spring wound up inside it real tight. And then it's got a little heat detector thing on the outside of it just like the one that's on the sprinkler head in most commercial buildings. And when there's enough heat, the thing pops off. Here the sprinklers would come off. In your house, this thing pops off and that metal spring that's wound up inside the can comes loose and unwinds. And it makes a racket that can be heard for miles because the metal is banging against metal as it's unwinding. Bang, bang, bang, bang, bang, bang, bang, bang, bang, bang, bang, bang, bang, bang, bang, and it wakes you up. That's a heat detector. And they're still expensive because they're sold by direct sales. So do your average household cost you about four grand to put in heats. So it's worth every penny because in two out of three fires, at least your house is going to burn to the ground if you live, if you're relying only on smokes. You can also wire the heats in now to your system if you want to so that they set off the alarm and they notify the fire department. They'll do anything you want them to do basically when the thing pops off. But that's how simple it is. If you had the little do-hickey, you could make it with a Maxwell House coffee can and a spring. The little do-hickey is what's magic, of course. But hardly anybody has heats unless somebody has come and sold them heats. You buy them from a guy in your house? They were in the house when you got them. Hardly anybody has. I mean, they're not even sold at Home Depot. You can't go to Home Depot and find them because somebody has to tell you this whole story and they also have to show you pictures of burnt kids and stuff in order to get you to buy them. Now, they just have to do the alarm system. Well, some of the real sophisticated alarm systems now have heat build-in. The reason why it's built-in is because two-thirds of the time it's going to be heat and not smoke. So, you mean the house still has a trigger for the smoke? No. But the thing is... No, no, no, no, no, no. There's only one company that makes the little do-hickey. They have a patent on it. That's it. Everybody's got to get the little do-hickey. Okay. If I had been lying, it would fall in this category. But still telling the truth, I just did something. I didn't invent it, but I made you aware of a problem you didn't know you had. And this is really what most selling is all about, right? I mean, we'd have a GDP of three if people only bought things they needed and already knew they needed. We'd all be living in a little hut and we'd ride to work on a bicycle and the liberals would be real happy with us because 90% of the shit you own, you really don't need. And you got it because somebody made you think you had a problem you don't have. And then they sold you something. So I mean, that's where we all make our livings. And Lasker's great line, oh, by the way, the Lasker book, if you don't read much advertising history, this one is worth reading. It's the man who sold America. And it's a definitive biography of Lasker. So Lasker said there is no law. I would, in parentheses, put yet. That requires a problem to exist before a solution is created for it and sold to cure it. And he's, of course, entirely right. And so when you write copy, you need not restrict yourself to preexisting problems. There's no requirement that says we have to do that. Nor do you have to deal with problems in perspective. You can take what is in perspective really a rather trivial matter and you can make it a major matter because that ties to what it is that you want to sell. So for example, this is, this is a current control. This is a 70-page direct mail piece. Looks like this. Self-mails. This is a, so it's a 70-page mag-a-log. And it sells a pill to take to help your sinuses. Now, I happen to have lifelong sinus trouble, but I will tell you on the list of my problems, it has never been on the top 10. But it gets a treatment here as if it was the worst medical problem on the planet. Right? I mean, it's just horrible. I mean, the Mayo Clinic for God's sake is involved in trying to solve it. All of the stuff you use that you buy at the drug store can kill you and has horrible side effects. Oh, I got to, oh, it'll destroy your immune system. So if you have occasional sinus problems, your whole immune system is going to hell and now you're going to get cancer and diabetes and heart disease and every other thing. The fact that you're tired is all because of your post-daisal drip. Right? So the fact that you need an apnea afternoon or can't get an erection or fall asleep during the basketball game is all because your sinuses are screwed up. There are 10,000 biological invaders that enter your nose every day and get trapped in there. So this is like the dust mite story. Right? You guys all know the dust mite story. You don't know this? Okay. So this is how, you'll see it in TV commercials if you pay attention. You don't watch TV, do you? Because you're 20. Yeah. So if you sell to somebody who's not 20, you might want to watch some TV. So the dust mite stories used to sell a new mattress every, how many years? Okay. Yeah. So your mattress doubles in weight every 10 years from these evil dust mites, which if you magnify them five million times, they look like giant cockroaches. Okay? Now they're actually the size of a, I don't know, a molecule. Right? A piece of dust. I mean, you know, but if you magnify them, they look like cockroaches. They're also used to sell like carpet cleaning and stuff because a carpet cleaner can clean your mattress and your couch. And the great picture, we're going to talk about pictures last. The great picture is a crawling baby on the bed with the blown up dust mite as big as the baby. Okay? That's like the great picture. His mom goes, holy shit. And the great vacuum cleaner demo is empty bag, clear bag, clear canister, and just use the vac against the mattress. Oh my God. There's all a year dead skin that came off over the years and there's these horrible dust mites and there's, you know, whatever else is in there. And it, including some stuff that actually was put in the mattress when it was made, chalk dust, God knows. And it all comes out and fills the canister in the vacuum cleaner and you say, I am never sleeping or having sex on that thing again until we solve this problem. It's horrible. Right? Well, this is the same deal. You know, this is 10,000 horrible. I mean, look at the size of these things. I mean, if 10,000 of these things are coming up inside your nose every day, your head's going to explode for Christ's sake. Right? I mean, and so this is 70 pages taking a, for most people, an utterly insignificant problem. In many cases, they didn't, it's so insignificant to them, they didn't really even know they had it. And now it is making it the most. See? So, here's for people, don't know they have it. Right? Sinus, this is, how are you saying it? Can sneak up on you. Bacteria secretly sneaks into your system. The eyes, nose, and mouth all provide openings where deadly biological invaders can sneak in. Right? So, this is two pages for people, they didn't even have a symptom. Now they do. Right? By the time you're done with this, you've got these symptoms. You now, oh, this is great. John Hopkins Hospital warns a bleeding brain and spinal fluid leaks. Dot, dot, dot. Down here and find prints somewhere. I'm sure they've disclaimed that if your nose drips once in a while, that's actually not going to cause the problem. Jesus, it also causes numbness in your teeth. How do your teeth feel? A serious outbreak of infections. It's great. Brilliant. We're retting. Yeah. No, no, no, no, no. Now, oh, this is good. Look at this picture. I mean, it's great. It's wonderful. And it ultimately gets to, I think it's a good, better, best offer, isn't it? Probably. Yeah. So, it's a good deal. So it's an inventory load because they know you're not going to reorder because the stuff doesn't do anything. So, by the way, that's a tip off in the alternative health industry. If they're not doing continuity and they're doing inventory load, you ain't going to feel a thing. I'm going to tell you right now. Maybe it does something, but you ain't going to feel it. So we better get the money while we can get it. So the best deal is a $206 load. The good deal is $126. And the trial offer, which is one little box, is $4690 and free reports and so forth. So, but think about this. There's 70 pages about occasional sinus issues. So there's no law that says your use of it needs to be proportional or reasonable in perspective to the actual impact of the problem in somebody's business or somebody's life. In fact, it shouldn't be. Whatever it is you're doing should be magnified in importance to be the number one destroyer, killer, evil threat, or if you must, opportunity, potential for gain in their life at this moment. That's where you need to get. I talked at Super Conference about the start of all this. I mean, this stuff, this is back to the turn of the century. It is one of these kinds of pitches. Mostly it was a guy standing on a crate next to a horse and wagon that came to town, but it was the same kind of deal. And in the 1920s, this kind of advertising really took off. People began to talk about bodily functions who did not. We never talked about bodily functions before. And by the way, once we let that out of Pandora's box, we are now to the point, I mean, a night of television is 30% talking about bodily functions. I really rather would not. I mean, the woman is dripping urine every place she's going. I can't get an erection. Really, I just wanted a half hour of comedy before I would. I just, you know, so it all started in the 20s, right? And it's not, Listerine was one of the first guys to do it. So Listerine was a failing product sold in very small quantities as a surgical antiseptic to hospitals. So they add writers for Listerine created bad breath. Nobody knew they had bad breath before. And of course, most people really didn't have bad breath. Now everybody's got bad breath. We all got it. And we all do all kinds of stuff for it. So you use mouth colored water three times a day. You've got little tongue strips you carry around in your pocket, which have been dipped in Listerine. I mean, it's a, but nobody knew. And apparently people were still getting pregnant. I mean, despite it, but so when they invented in 1922, they invented bad breath. And in one year, their ad spend went from virtually zero because all they were doing was selling this stuff to hospitals to five million a year. This is 1922, 23, five million a year. Who's in the financial services? Larry, what's five million dollars in 2016 money? You're supposed to know the multiple. You're in the business. What's the matter with you? Lang, where are you? Come on, my financial people. Really? Nobody knows? Okay, so it's $200 million. Right? In ad spend. Right? In one year from zero by creating bad breath. They were in 80 magazines, 300 newspapers, and they produced a profit of $4 million on that product. And they did it with you. I think you have, yeah, you got these ads. So I'm like on page 11. Right? Don't fool yourself that you never have halitosis has do so many self-assured. What's that? What's that paragraph from yesterday? Thank you. It's gaslighting. That's exactly right. Don't fool yourself that you never have halitosis has do so many self-assured people who constantly offend this way. Right? Because, you know, at this point, we're just inventing bad breath. Nobody thinks they got it. Right? And while people talk to me, I just think, I get laid, I sell shit. What are you talking about? So we got to make them unsure of themselves. Here's a demo. Put down in the lower right corner. Here's demonstration. Right? Rub a slice of onion on your hand, then apply listerine. Immediately, every trace of onion odor is gone. They got a demo in here. So if you go back to yesterday's list and you take a look at the samples when you get home, you can find a lot of yesterday's list. This is one of their better ones. Could I be happy with him in spite of that? And 14, often a bridesmaid, but never a bride. Right? If only she didn't have bad breath. Might stop a guy from marrying her. It's not going to stop her from having an active sex life, but it's going to stop... Athlete's Foot. It was invented in 1930 by a copywriter. And the first product sold for a treatment for Athlete's Foot was Absorbing Junior, the liniment, which is diluted DMSO, I think. The liniment, we use it on horses. You used it for arthritis pain. Guess what? It also cures Athlete's Foot. Body odor was created by the John Powers ad agency or by Ruff and Ryan, another ad agency. There's an argument about who got there first in 1928. None of these came from the medical world. They came from the advertising world. They were a solution for a problem that did not yet exist, at least in anybody's minds or awareness. Some people, by the way, will ask me about business niches. Somebody asked me yesterday if it was either real estate or dentistry, if there was room for another, you know, profit, another guru, another advisor. And one answer to that is always, if you can figure out a new problem that they didn't know they had, that nobody else is fixing, of course there's room for you. And there's a world full of problems that can be created. Number three, part of my answer to you this morning. See, I told, oh, Ron's not there. So stacking. So in copy, the order in which we do things matters, just as it does in a one-to-one sales presentation. The order in which we do things matters, and it can matter a lot. And so how you figure out what order you're going to put things in is at least as important as what it is you choose to say or write. And so my process, again, is to shuffle the deck. So my process is to have a single sentence, a single paragraph, a headline, a subhead, whatever, every one of them on a single sheet so I can keep reordering them and reordering them and reordering them until I have the raw copy in an order to now go back and stitch it together and bridge it and finish it and write it. So this is from, I think it's from our last call, Jim. So you'll get the first one probably easily. You may not get the second one so easily. So if you have to make a choice, which of these should be the first one?
